name: Tiwanaku Origins
description: The origins of the Tiwanaku civilization, centered near Lake Titicaca in Bolivia, are shrouded in mystery, leading to various theories beyond mainstream archaeological interpretations. Mainstream archaeology places the rise of Tiwanaku between 300 and 1000 CE, with evidence of earlier settlements. Alternative theories often propose a much older age, sometimes tens of thousands of years, attributing the city's construction to pre-diluvian civilizations, extraterrestrial influence, or unknown advanced technologies. Proponents point to the precise stonework, astronomical alignments, and apparent engineering feats as evidence of capabilities beyond those attributed to the Tiwanaku people by mainstream scholars. They suggest that the city was built by a more advanced culture and later inhabited or repurposed by the Tiwanaku. These theories often incorporate elements of catastrophism, claiming a global cataclysm destroyed the original builders, leaving behind the ruins for the Tiwanaku to adapt. Mainstream archaeology refutes these claims, citing evidence of gradual development and adaptation of existing technologies, as well as the lack of definitive proof for a pre-diluvian civilization or extraterrestrial intervention. They emphasize the importance of understanding the cultural and environmental context of Tiwanaku, highlighting the ingenuity of the Tiwanaku people in adapting to the high-altitude environment and developing complex social and political structures. Radiocarbon dating and stratigraphy support the mainstream timeline, placing the major construction phases within the established timeframe. While acknowledging unanswered questions and ongoing research, mainstream scholars maintain that the current evidence best supports a gradual development of Tiwanaku by the Tiwanaku people themselves.
subtitle: Ancient city's contested beginnings.

claims.claim: Tiwanaku is much older than mainstream archaeology suggests.
claims.evidence: Precise stonework, astronomical alignments, claims of advanced engineering, disputed dating methods.
claims.criticism: Radiocarbon dating and stratigraphy support a later timeframe. Precision can be explained by skilled craftsmanship and iterative design.

claims.claim: Tiwanaku was built by a pre-diluvian civilization or extraterrestrial beings.
claims.evidence: Alleged evidence of advanced technology beyond the capabilities of the Tiwanaku people, similarities to other ancient sites worldwide.
claims.criticism: Lack of concrete evidence of advanced technology or extraterrestrial intervention. Similarities can be attributed to convergent evolution of technology or cultural exchange.

claims.claim: Tiwanaku was destroyed by a global cataclysm.
claims.evidence: Geological evidence of past environmental changes, legends of floods and disasters, perceived signs of damage to the city.
claims.criticism: Evidence suggests gradual decline due to environmental factors and social unrest, rather than a sudden catastrophic event. Damage can be explained by natural processes and human activity over time.

keyFigures.name: Arthur Posnansky
keyFigures.role: Early researcher who proposed a very early date for Tiwanaku.

keyFigures.name: Alan Kolata
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist specializing in Tiwanaku, providing mainstream interpretations.

keyFigures.name: Graham Hancock
keyFigures.role: Author promoting alternative theories about ancient civilizations, including Tiwanaku.

timeline.event: Formative Period - Early settlements and development of agriculture.
Classic Period - Rise of Tiwanaku as a major political and religious center.
Decline of Tiwanaku - Possible environmental factors and social unrest.
Rise of alternative theories about Tiwanaku's origins and purpose.

sources.author: Posnansky, Arthur
sources.work: Tihuanacu: The Cradle of American Man
sources.title: Posnansky, Arthur
